text.title: Objection Bank Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every objection-bank entry and update must satisfy. The bank is a living artifact; these rules keep it useful past the first month.

rule.statement: Every demo call note, cold-outreach reply log, Product Hunt comment thread, and customer-support ticket ends with an `objections-mentioned` field listing zero-or-more objection IDs (or `none` if none surfaced). The field is mandatory; the note cannot be filed without it.

rationale.source: information-capture friction studies; CRM data-hygiene research

rule.rationale: Objections caught at the moment of utterance carry the exact phrasing, the trigger context, and (often) the implied price-or-trust dimension. Reconstructed-from-memory objections lose the load-bearing detail. Mandatory-field-at-source is the only capture pattern that survives a busy week.

example.title: Encounter log footer
example.code:
## Call notes — 2026-05-19 — Acme prospect (J. Smith, head of platform) ... call discussion ...
### Objections mentioned - price-vs-llm-spend-rounding (severity: 4/5; quote: "we already pay $4k/mo to Anthropic, your $99 is rounding error so I want to be sure the value is concrete") - security-soc2-status (severity: 5/5; quote: "we cannot sign before SOC2 Type 2")

rule.statement: Each objection has a `score = encounter_count * blocked_revenue_estimate`. Encounter count is incremented at source. Blocked-revenue is the sum of the deal/MRR each objection blocked or delayed. Re-rank the top-20 weekly; this is the input to landing-copy decisions.
rule.rationale: Pure frequency over-weights cheap, low-revenue customers. Pure revenue-impact over-weights one-off lost whales. The product captures both signals and orders the bank by what is actually leaking money.

rule.statement: Every entry in the top-10 must have a `resolved_in` field naming the landing-page section, FAQ entry, or pricing-page row that answers the objection. If the value is `unanswered`, a copy-update task is auto-filed in the marketing backlog and surfaces in the monthly diff post.
rule.rationale: An objection that is captured but never tied back to copy is shelfware. The whole point of the bank is to drive landing-page edits; the `resolved_in` field is the feedback signal that closes the loop.

rule.statement: On the first Friday of each month, the founder publishes a one-page diff post in the marketing channel: top-3 movers (rising in rank), top-3 fallers, any new entries from the past month, the unanswered-on-copy list. The post is the trigger for the monthly landing review.
rule.rationale: Without a forced surfacing event, the bank rots into a write-only file. The monthly diff is short enough to read in 2 minutes and load-bearing enough that skipping it has visible consequences (objections continue to bleed deals).

rule.statement: Each entry has exactly one canonical `counter_statement` (40-80 words). It is the version used in demos, cold replies, and landing copy. Variations and tone-shifts are recorded as `variants`, never replacing the canonical. The canonical is reviewed quarterly.
rule.rationale: A single canonical counter forces the founder to commit to one position and lets new collaborators (contractors, VAs) be onboarded in one read. Multiple counter-statements proliferate and dilute the position; without a canonical, demo calls drift into improvisation.

list.item: security — SOC2, GDPR, on-prem, data residency
integration — does it work with our existing stack
fit — buyer is not the right fit but is exploring anyway
